adoption_interest.interested_in: Adult mixed-breed dog (medium size, 30–50 lbs) that is good with other dogs and enjoys hiking
adoption_interest.name: Melissa Ann Carpenter

pet_preferences.tell_us_more_line_1: I am looking for a friendly, medium-energy adult dog who enjoys daily walks and weekend hikes but can also settle nicely in the house. I run 3–4 miles a few times a week and would love a dog that can eventually jog with me once we build up their stamina safely and with our vet’s approval. I’m not looking for a super high-drive working breed that needs constant intense activity, but I do want a dog that genuinely enjoys being active and engaged with their people.
pet_preferences.tell_us_more_line_2: Temperament-wise, I really value a dog who is people-social, enjoys meeting visitors, and can handle living in a neighborhood setting where we regularly pass other dogs and people on sidewalks and trails. My current dog, Jasper (a 6-year-old neutered male Lab mix), is very dog-social but can be a little insecure with pushy dogs, so I’d like a dog who has good canine social skills and is not overly reactive or rough in play. I’m fine continuing training and working with a trainer if needed; I’ve done positive reinforcement classes before and enjoy that process.
pet_preferences.tell_us_more_line_3: I live with my partner and we often have our nieces (ages 6 and 9) visit on weekends and during school breaks. Because of that, I’d prefer a dog who either has a known history of doing well with respectful children or at least does not have a history of aggression or significant fear toward kids. I’m very proactive about management—I supervise all interactions, teach kids how to give dogs space, and make sure the dog always has a quiet area to retreat to—but I want to set everyone up for success from the start.
pet_preferences.tell_us_more_line_4: I’m also open to a dog who may need some basic training or confidence-building, such as leash manners, learning to settle, or working through mild separation anxiety. I work part-time from home and have the flexibility to do gradual alone-time training. I am not currently looking for a dog with severe behavioral issues like a bite history toward people, but I am absolutely open to mild to moderate quirks (e.g., some leash reactivity, nervousness in new environments) and am willing to invest in professional training and enrichment toys, puzzle feeders, and structured exercise to help the dog thrive.

discussion_points.additional_topics_or_questions_line_1: I would love some specific guidance on the best way to introduce a new dog to our existing dog, Jasper, beyond the general advice I’ve read online. For example, do you recommend a particular neutral meeting spot close to the shelter, and is it possible to do more than one meet-and-greet with the same dog on different days to make sure they are really a good fit? Jasper tends to be polite at first but can get overwhelmed if another dog is extremely high-energy right away, so I want to go slowly and set up several positive, short interactions if that’s an option.
discussion_points.additional_topics_or_questions_line_2: I’m also curious about any known medical history for the dogs I’m considering—especially orthopedic issues, allergies, or previous surgeries—because I’m prepared for routine vet care but want to be realistic about any long-term conditions or medications. If a dog has mild, manageable medical needs (like a special diet or joint supplements), I’m open to that, but I’d appreciate a clear explanation of what to expect financially and in terms of daily care over the next several years.
discussion_points.additional_topics_or_questions_line_3: In addition, I’d like recommendations for local positive-reinforcement trainers or group classes in the Eugene area that you trust and have had good feedback about from adopters. We did a basic manners class with Jasper when we first adopted him, and I’d like to enroll a new dog in something similar fairly quickly—both for training and for structured socialization. If you have any printed materials or online resources you give new adopters, I’d be happy to read through those as well.
discussion_points.additional_topics_or_questions_line_4: Could you also let me know what your typical post-adoption support looks like? For example, if we encounter a behavior issue in the first few weeks (like mild resource guarding or separation-related behaviors), is there someone on staff we can email or call for guidance before we schedule an outside trainer? I really value having a support network and want to make sure I’m using all the resources available to keep the adoption successful.
discussion_points.additional_topics_or_questions_line_5: Finally, I’m interested in understanding your return or foster-to-adopt policies in more detail—not because I plan to give up on a dog, but because I want to be prepared in case there is a serious, unforeseen incompatibility with Jasper or our home environment. Knowing in advance how you handle trial periods, follow-up check-ins, and any required steps if things truly don’t work out will help me feel more confident about choosing a dog that may need a bit of extra time and patience to settle in.
